edges.description: NAPQI-protein adducts and glutathione depletion converge on mitochondrial injury.
edges.source: NAPQI reactive metabolite formation and glutathione depletion

edges.description: Necrotic hepatocytes release damage-associated signals that recruit inflammatory cells.
Massive hepatocyte loss produces acute liver failure.
Sustained inflammatory injury extends hepatocyte loss toward liver failure.

nodes.description: Fulminant hepatic failure from massive hepatocyte loss, the most severe outcome of acetaminophen overdose.

evidence.snippet: Acetaminophen (APAP) alone accounts for the half of the overall cases of acute liver failure in USA

nodes.description: Mitochondrial failure and oxidative stress precipitate predominantly necrotic death of centrilobular hepatocytes, the central effector lesion of acetaminophen hepatotoxicity and of the DILI module.

evidence.snippet: the most common manifestation is hepatocyte death following drug intake

evidence.reference_title: Recommendations for the use of the acetaminophen hepatotoxicity model for mechanistic studies and how to avoid common pitfalls.
evidence.snippet: the critical roles of drug metabolism, mitochondrial dysfunction, necrotic cell death, autophagy and the sterile inflammatory response

nodes.description: NAPQI adduct formation and glutathione depletion provoke mitochondrial oxidative stress, impaired respiration, and the mitochondrial permeability transition — the major cellular event linking acetaminophen exposure to hepatocyte death.

evidence.reference_title: The molecular mechanisms of acetaminophen-induced hepatotoxicity and its potential therapeutic targets.
evidence.snippet: Mitochondrial oxidative stress and dysfunction are the major cellular events associated with APAP-induced liver injury.

nodes.description: In overdose, cytochrome P450 metabolism of acetaminophen generates the reactive electrophile N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI), which depletes hepatocyte glutathione and binds cellular and mitochondrial proteins — the proximal toxic trigger that the DILI module's reactive-metabolite node represents.
nodes.id: NAPQI reactive metabolite formation and glutathione depletion

evidence.reference_title: The molecular mechanisms of acetaminophen-induced hepatotoxicity and its potential therapeutic targets.
evidence.snippet: APAP-induced hepatotoxicity (AIH) is mainly caused by the toxic metabolite N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI)

nodes.description: Necrotic hepatocytes release damage-associated molecular patterns that drive a sterile inflammatory response, recruiting innate immune cells that amplify and extend the hepatocellular injury.

evidence.reference_title: Recommendations for the use of the acetaminophen hepatotoxicity model for mechanistic studies and how to avoid common pitfalls.
evidence.snippet: the critical roles of drug metabolism, mitochondrial dysfunction, necrotic cell death, autophagy and the sterile inflammatory response
